intro.audience: A busy Durham homeowner who clicked an ad or searched house cleaning near me.
intro.job: Earn enough trust in 10 seconds that they book a first clean instead of bouncing to call three competitors.

notes.label: Hero
notes.what: The hero leads with trust (a key) and an instant price — not a phone number.
notes.why: This buyer is letting a stranger into their home at 11pm; leading with the 60-second price and the W-2/insured signals answers both the 'what will it cost' and 'is this safe' fears before the first scroll.
notes.before: The old hero ran a generic 'we clean homes' headline over a phone number, so a ready-to-book visitor had to call and wait for a callback.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: Trust bar
notes.what: A trust bar of the four biggest objections — insured, W-2, background-checked, guaranteed — sits right under the hero.
notes.why: Doubt about who's coming spikes the instant they consider booking, so the vetting cues land exactly there instead of waiting for an About page nobody reaches.
notes.before: The W-2 and background-check story lived in a footer line nobody read, so Marigold's most expensive, most defensible asset stayed invisible.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 1 · Where you are

notes.label: Services
notes.what: Services are framed around the rhythm of a home, with the recurring plan leading and each card written outcome-first.
notes.why: Recurring is the high-LTV product for a dual-income household that wants to stop thinking about cleaning, so it leads and sells the result ('always ready') rather than the chore ('we dust').
notes.before: The old site listed one-time and recurring cleans as equals on a flat services grid, training demand toward the lowest-value, highest-churn product.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: How it works
notes.what: A 3-step how-it-works shows the whole job done in 60 seconds.
notes.why: The friction that kills cleaning leads is call-and-wait-for-a-quote; showing a short self-serve path reassures the time-poor buyer that booking won't cost them an evening.
notes.before: The old path was a contact form and a 24–48h callback loop, which lost the impulse of a visitor deciding late at night.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

notes.label: Pricing
notes.what: Transparent flat per-visit pricing is published, with the every-2-weeks plan highlighted.
notes.why: Price transparency is rare in this category and reads as confidence, not commodity; highlighting one plan anchors the busy professional to the stickiest, most profitable rhythm.
notes.before: The old site said 'call for a quote,' which reads as 'we'll size you up,' repelling the professional who'd happily pay $149 to never think about it.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: Guarantee
notes.what: The 24-hour re-clean guarantee is stated as risk reversal, backed by the 94% retention stat.
notes.why: It removes the last switching risk ('what if they do a bad job') for someone trying a new team, and the retention number proves the promise is real, not marketing.
notes.before: The old site made no guarantee promise anywhere in the booking path, leaving the 'what if it's bad' fear unanswered at the moment of commitment.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

notes.label: Reviews
notes.what: Specific, named reviews span different customer types.
notes.why: Concrete details ('they know my dog') read as real to a trust-anxious buyer, and spanning recurring/host/move-out lets every visitor find someone like themselves.
notes.before: The old site leaned on a bare star count with no named, specific stories, so the proof read as asserted rather than earned.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: Closing CTA
notes.what: One closing CTA repeats the single booking action with the guarantee restated.
notes.why: Ending on one unambiguous action, not a footer wall, keeps the high-intent visitor on the one path the whole page points to.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

intro.audience: Busy professional and dual-income Durham households who want one reliable cleaning team on a standing schedule — the high-LTV recurring customer, not a one-time deep-clean shopper.
intro.job: Make the recurring plan (especially every-2-weeks) feel like the obvious default: show an instant per-visit price up front, prove the same-team trust position, remove the perceived risk of a 'subscription,' and convert the visitor into a booked recurring plan.

notes.label: Price hero
notes.what: An instant-price hero presets the quote card to 2–3 bd / Every 2 weeks at $149/visit, with a hands-off-home headline and trust signals (4.9, same team, cancel anytime).
notes.why: Leading with a concrete number removes the call-and-wait friction and anchors the every-2-weeks plan as the reference choice before any objection forms.
notes.before: The old site gave no price and no instant quote here, so a recurring-ready visitor had to phone in and wait while the impulse cooled.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: Trust bar
notes.what: A trust bar restates same-team-every-visit, W-2 / background-checked, free skip/reschedule, and the 24-hour re-clean guarantee.
notes.why: Surfacing the credentials right under the hero counters the 'rotating gig-app stranger' fear that specifically blocks committing to a standing plan.
notes.before: The W-2 and vetting story was buried in a footer, so the one thing that makes a standing relationship feel safe was the hardest thing to find.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: Why recurring
notes.what: Three benefit cards (a team that knows your home, it never falls behind, you get weekends back) sit beside the 94%-retention stat.
notes.why: It frames a one-time clean as effort that fades versus a plan that compounds, and the retention stat signals this is the safe, popular choice for households like theirs.
notes.before: The old flat services grid treated recurring as just another line item, never making the case for why the standing plan is the better deal.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: How it works
notes.what: Four numbered steps (pick rhythm, meet team, it repeats automatically, come home to clean) end in a primary CTA.
notes.why: Making setup feel like a 3-minute, self-running process lowers activation energy and the fear of being locked into something complicated.
notes.before: The old contact-form-and-callback path made even starting a recurring plan feel like a phone-tag chore.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

notes.label: What's included
notes.what: A what's-included-every-visit checklist of 8 itemized tasks sits beside a checklist visual, with an eco/fragrance-free note.
notes.why: Enumerating exactly what every recurring visit delivers makes the per-visit price feel fair and pre-empts the 'recurring just means a rushed wipe-down' objection.
notes.before: The old site listed tasks as a generic service description, not as proof of what each standing visit reliably includes for the price.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: Flexibility band
notes.what: A sage band on flexibility and no-contracts (skip, pull forward, or pause free) pairs a $0 / 24-hr / No-contracts stat trio with the guarantee shield.
notes.why: It directly disarms commitment anxiety — the biggest barrier to subscribing — by proving the plan flexes and is guaranteed, making 'yes' low-stakes.
notes.before: The old site framed nothing about flexibility or guarantees, so 'recurring' read as a contract trap to a cautious buyer.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

notes.label: Pricing tiers
notes.what: Three plan cards (Monthly $189, Every-2-weeks $149 featured 'Most popular', Weekly $129) flank and emphasize the every-2-weeks option.
notes.why: Flanking the featured plan with a pricier and a cheaper option steers choice to the high-LTV every-2-weeks default, while 'less per visit the more often' nudges frequency up.
notes.before: The old 'call for a quote' approach hid pricing entirely, so the buyer couldn't compare plans or be steered toward the best-value rhythm.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: Recurring reviews
notes.what: Three recurring-client testimonials (every-2-weeks and weekly households) emphasize same-team familiarity, stress removed, and easy skipping.
notes.why: Matching reviewers to the exact target household lets the visitor see someone like them succeeding with the precise plan being sold.
notes.before: The old site's generic reviews never spoke to the standing-plan customer, so a recurring shopper saw no proof aimed at their decision.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: Closing CTA
notes.what: A closing CTA band ('Make a clean home your new default') pairs a gold primary book button with a secondary link to reviews.
notes.why: A benefit-led close with one dominant CTA reduces decision paralysis at the moment of highest intent, while the soft secondary keeps the not-yet-ready inside the funnel.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

intro.audience: Busy professional / dual-income Durham households ready to book, plus realtors and short-term-rental hosts who need a reliable team — visitors who have decided they want a cleaner and are now deciding whether to trust and commit to this one.
intro.job: Convert intent into a booking. Turn the home page's single primary CTA into a finished action: show a confident, multi-step booking + instant-price flow, surround it with trust reinforcement (insured, W-2, background-checked, same team), remove perceived risk (no card today, no contract, free reschedule, 24-hour re-clean guarantee), and answer the last-mile objections that stall a booking.

notes.label: Booking flow
notes.what: A conversion hero pairs action copy ('See your price and book your first clean') with a no-card/no-contract checklist and an enlarged 4-step booking widget (Home / Frequency / Day / Price) that shows a live $149 price and a 'Confirm & book' CTA.
notes.why: Putting the actual instant-price tool above the fold lets the visitor self-qualify and see the price is the price; the visible step rail uses the goal-gradient effect, where showing the finish line is near increases completion.
notes.before: The old site had no booking flow at all — every lead hit a contact form and a 24–48h callback, the single biggest leak of booking-ready demand.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

notes.label: Reassurance strip
notes.what: A slim trust strip under the hero repeats the four proof points (insured & bonded, W-2 not gig, background-checked, 24-hour guarantee) with inline icons.
notes.why: A cleaner is a stranger you let into your home, so reinforcing safety credentials right beneath the booking widget lowers the perceived risk exactly as the visitor considers committing.
notes.before: The old site kept its vetting credentials in a footer, far from any place a booking decision actually happened.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 1 · Where you are

notes.label: Why confident
notes.what: A four-card grid expands each trust pillar (insured & bonded, W-2 employees, background-checked, the same team) with a sentence of substance on what each guarantees.
notes.why: The W-2/background-check story is Marigold's wedge against gig apps, so spelling it out here counters the visitor's strongest objection ('is this just another marketplace?') with concrete reasons.
notes.before: On the old site this wedge was a buried footer line, so the most defensible advantage did none of the selling.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: What happens next
notes.what: Three numbered steps describe the post-booking experience: confirm online (instant email with team name + arrival window), we prep & arrive, you come home to clean with the re-clean guarantee.
notes.why: Fear of the unknown after clicking 'book' (sales call? long form? when do they show up?) causes hesitation, so showing a short, calm, fully-online path makes committing feel safe and effortless.
notes.before: The old callback model left everything after the inquiry a mystery — when someone would call back, what they'd ask, and when anyone would actually show up.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

notes.label: Plan recap
notes.what: The three recurring plans (Monthly $189, Every-2-weeks $149 featured 'Most popular', Weekly $129) are restated as bookable cards, each with a direct book CTA and a link to compare plans.
notes.why: Flagging the middle option most-popular nudges choice toward the high-LTV biweekly plan, and confirming flat, contract-free pricing at the moment of choice removes the ambiguity that would send the visitor back to comparison-shop.
notes.before: The old site never published pricing, so a ready buyer couldn't confirm what they'd pay and would leave to compare quotes elsewhere.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: Guarantee band
notes.what: A deep-green band states the 24-hour re-clean promise with a 'Book without the risk' headline and three stat tiles (24h re-clean window, $0 to book today, 100% background-checked).
notes.why: Explicitly transferring the risk to the business ('if it's not right, we re-clean it free, $0 today') is the classic closer that lets a hesitant visitor say yes, and the $0 stat neutralizes payment anxiety at the conversion moment.
notes.before: The old site offered no guarantee and asked for commitment before any price, so the risk sat entirely on the nervous buyer.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

notes.label: Booking FAQ
notes.what: A four-card FAQ handles the last-mile objections: do I have to be home, is there a contract, supplies/products, when am I charged.
notes.why: These are the precise doubts that cause abandonment in home services, so answering them inline (no contract, nothing charged today, secure entry with the same trusted team) clears the final blockers before the closing CTA.
notes.before: The old site forced these unanswered questions onto a phone call, so any one of them could stall or kill a booking with no recourse on the page.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

notes.label: Closing CTA
notes.what: A closing CTA band ('Your price is one minute away') pairs a primary gold 'See my price & book' button with a softer 'Read the reviews first' secondary link.
notes.why: Repeating one unambiguous CTA reinforces the site-wide focus, while the low-commitment 'read reviews' secondary keeps a still-hesitant visitor inside the funnel via social proof rather than bouncing.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

intro.audience: Prospective Marigold clients — busy professional households evaluating whether to hand a recurring cleaning team a key to their home — plus realtors and STR hosts doing trust due-diligence before they book.
intro.job: Convert trust-stage visitors by making the people and the vetting tangible: tell the founder's hotel-housekeeping origin story, prove the W-2 (not gig) decision matters, lay out the vetting and insurance, and land the same-team promise — so the visitor feels safe enough to book.

notes.label: Hero
notes.what: The headline 'The people you'd trust with a key' carries the founder/team framing, a trio illustration, two floating trust chips, dual CTAs, and a 4.9-star row.
notes.why: It continues the home page's signature 'trust with a key' line so the page feels like one promise kept, and leads with faces and proof rather than features to set a human frame.
notes.before: The old About page led with generic 'we clean homes' copy that claimed no position and tied to nothing on the rest of the site.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: Trust bar
notes.what: A compact icon bar states 14 W-2 cleaners, 100% background-checked, insured & bonded, owner-led since 2017.
notes.why: Hard credentials placed immediately below the hero reduce perceived risk before the visitor invests reading time, carrying over the home page's trust-bar pattern.
notes.before: The old site mentioned these credentials only in a footer line, so the proof a nervous buyer needed never sat where they'd see it.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 1 · Where you are

notes.label: Founder story
notes.what: Renata Cole's story — nine years running boutique-hotel housekeeping, why she started Marigold in 2017 — runs beside an RC avatar and closes with 'she still walks every new client's first clean.'
notes.why: A named, credentialed human with a hotel-grade pedigree makes the standard believable and likable (people trust people, not logos), and the personal-involvement detail signals accountability.
notes.before: The old site was faceless brochure-ware with no founder, no origin, and no human reason to trust the standard.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: W-2 vs gig
notes.what: A two-column contrast card sets 'The Marigold way (W-2)' in brand green against a greyed-out 'gig-app way,' four points each (accountability, same team, insurance, retention vs churn).
notes.why: Positioning against the gig-app alternative sharpens the unique value and reframes the higher price as the cost of trust, making the W-2 choice feel obviously safer.
notes.before: The old site never contrasted itself with gig apps, so its expensive W-2 advantage looked like an unexplained higher price.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: Vetting process
notes.what: A six-card grid lays out the hiring and vetting process: background check, training to one standard, references & interview, insured & bonded, ongoing reviews, treated-like-teammates.
notes.why: Turning 'trustworthy' into a visible, repeatable process answers the unspoken 'who exactly is coming into my home?' objection that blocks the booking.
notes.before: On the old site 'background-checked' was a single footer claim with no process behind it, so it read as a slogan rather than proof.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 1 · Where you are

notes.label: Same-team promise
notes.what: A deep-green band states the same-team promise — matched to a small named team, no re-explaining, covered if your regular is out — with a figures illustration and a link to recurring plans.
notes.why: It sells the continuity gig apps structurally can't promise and routes the high-LTV recurring buyer toward the recurring product.
notes.before: The old site sold a one-off transaction and never named the same-team relationship that is Marigold's actual product.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: Values
notes.what: Four value cards — trust first, consistency over flash, people we'd hire twice, make it right — embed the 24-hour re-clean guarantee.
notes.why: Stated principles signal integrity, and the embedded re-clean guarantee provides risk reversal exactly when the visitor is weighing whether the team will deliver.
notes.before: The old site stated no values and made no guarantee, leaving the 'will they actually deliver' question unanswered.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

notes.label: Team voices
notes.what: Three testimonial-style cards from named cleaners, tenure shown, explain why they stay and how they care for repeat homes.
notes.why: Hearing the team's pride and long tenure validates the retention claim and humanizes the workers, making the visitor more comfortable letting them in.
notes.before: The old site treated cleaners as anonymous labor, so there was no evidence the people behind the W-2 claim actually stay.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: Closing CTA
notes.what: A closing gradient CTA band ('Meet a team you'll actually want to keep') restates the 60-second booking promise and the guarantee, with a gold primary CTA and a reviews link.
notes.why: A low-friction, time-bounded ask plus the restated guarantee removes the final hesitation and converts the trust just built into a booking.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan

intro.audience: Prospective Durham-area clients in the evaluation stage — busy households, realtors, short-term-rental hosts, and post-reno homeowners who are weighing Marigold against gig-app services and need proof before they hand over a key.
intro.job: Convert consideration into a booking by presenting social proof at scale: lead with the headline rating (4.9 / 300+), then deliver specific, credible testimonials segmented by customer type so every visitor sees someone like themselves, and close on the booking CTA backed by the guarantee.

notes.label: Rating summary
notes.what: A hero shows the 4.9 headline rating, a verified-review count, and a star-distribution breakdown (93% five-star) beside the primary book CTA.
notes.why: A high average over a large, visible sample sets a trust anchor in the first viewport, while the distribution makes the score feel audited rather than asserted.
notes.before: The old site showed at most a bare star number with no sample size or breakdown, so the rating looked claimed rather than earned.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: Proof stats
notes.what: A trustbar strip restates 4.9 average, 300+ reviews, 94% retention past six months, and that most reviews mention 'the same team.'
notes.why: Compressing the headline proof points into scannable badges reinforces credibility for skimmers and previews the page's core differentiator, consistency.
notes.before: The old site never surfaced retention or review volume, so the very numbers that prove the same-team moat went unsaid.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 1 · Where you are

notes.label: Segments
notes.what: A four-card grid names the customer segments (recurring households, realtors/move-outs, STR hosts, post-reno), each with its own star average and review volume.
notes.why: Visitors persuade themselves fastest when they spot their exact situation, so segmenting proof lets each persona route to evidence that speaks to their use case.
notes.before: The old site lumped all reviews together, so a realtor or host couldn't tell whether anyone like them had been served well.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: Featured review
notes.what: A single large, highlighted 'most-helpful' testimonial carries rich narrative detail (named client, neighborhood, two-year tenure, the folded-blanket moment).
notes.why: One concrete, emotionally specific story is more persuasive than many generic ones and models the depth of relationship the brand is selling.
notes.before: The old site had only short, generic blurbs, so nothing conveyed the lived, long-term relationship that is Marigold's product.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: Recurring reviews
notes.what: A six-card testimonial grid from recurring clients covers same-team familiarity, transparent pricing, the notes-follow-the-home detail, trust while away, and a guarantee-honored story.
notes.why: Volume plus specificity (named neighborhoods, plan cadence, concrete objections answered) makes the proof read as real and pre-empts the top buying hesitations for the high-LTV segment.
notes.before: The old site never grouped proof by the recurring household, so its most valuable buyer saw nothing aimed at their decision.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: Realtor reviews
notes.what: Three detailed testimonials from agents and movers cover deadline reliability, deposit-back move-outs, and photograph-ready results.
notes.why: Peers vouching for on-the-day reliability and documented results address this segment's single biggest fear — a clean that misses the close.
notes.before: The old site offered no segment-specific proof, so a realtor evaluating a vendor for a hard deadline had nothing to go on.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: Host reviews
notes.what: Three testimonials from Airbnb/VRBO hosts cover same-day turnovers, photo checklists, and a flat 5.0 cleanliness rating.
notes.why: Hosts fear bad guest reviews and missed check-ins, so proof of zero-miss turnovers and protected ratings reframes the service as insurance for their income.
notes.before: The old site never spoke to STR hosts at all, so the turnover-dependent buyer saw no evidence Marigold could be trusted with checkout timing.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 3 · Customers worth choosing

notes.label: Reno reviews
notes.what: Three post-renovation testimonials include a contractor referral and one honest four-star review about a second pass.
notes.why: A deliberately included non-perfect review and a pro endorsement raise the believability of the whole wall, since flawless five-star sets read as curated or fake.
notes.before: The old site showed only uniform glowing blurbs, which read as cherry-picked and lowered trust in every review.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: Review themes
notes.what: A sage band summarizes the four words clients use most — same team (71%), trust (58%), reliable (44%), thorough (39%).
notes.why: Quantifying the recurring themes does the reader's pattern-matching for them and converts a stack of anecdotes into a defensible, memorable positioning claim.
notes.before: The old site left reviews as scattered anecdotes, so the consistency theme that is the brand's whole position never got named.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 2 · The position only you can take

notes.label: Closing CTA
notes.what: A closing CTA band ('Become the next 4.9★ review') restates the 60-second booking promise and the 24-hour re-clean guarantee with primary and recurring-plans buttons.
notes.why: Placing the ask immediately after peak proof captures earned trust, and restating the guarantee lowers the perceived cost of saying yes.
notes.bookLabel: Ch. 4 · The rebuilt site and the 90-day plan
